title: Scalable self-testing of generic multipartite quantum states
authors: Jinchang Liu, Elias X. Huber, Zhenyu Du, Xingjian Zhang, Xiongfeng Ma
abstract: Characterizing large quantum systems with minimal assumptions is a central challenge in quantum information science. Self-testing provides the strongest form of certification by identifying the underlying quantum state solely from observed measurement statistics. However, existing self-testing methods for generic $n$-partite states face a scalability barrier, requiring exponentially many samples in the system size. In this work, we overcome this barrier by introducing a protocol that robustly self-tests almost all $n$-qubit states with only polynomial sample complexity. The key ingredient is an efficient scheme for device-independently evaluating multipartite Pauli measurements, which can be implemented using only a linear number of ancillary Bell pairs together with standard projective and Bell measurements, well within the reach of current quantum technology. Beyond self-testing states, our scheme provides a general framework for implementing a wide range of learning and certification protocols in the device-independent setting, thereby opening a scalable route to device-independent quantum information processing in large-scale quantum networks.
summary: This paper presents a scalable protocol for the device-independent self-testing of generic multipartite quantum states. By overcoming the exponential sample complexity barrier and resolving measurement sign inconsistencies, it enables the efficient certification of large-scale quantum networks using only polynomial resources.
main_problem: Existing self-testing methods for generic n-partite quantum states suffer from an exponential scalability barrier, requiring an exponential number of samples relative to the system size.
main_result: The authors introduce a protocol that robustly self-tests almost all n-qubit states with only polynomial sample complexity, overcoming the local-transpose obstacle.
method: The protocol uses a transpose-braiding test to ensure global consistency of Pauli measurements and a teleportation-based scheme using a linear number of ancillary Bell pairs to evaluate multipartite Pauli measurements.
model_or_system: An n-qubit multipartite quantum system shared among n main parties and n auxiliary parties, utilizing a network-assisted architecture with shared Bell pairs.
key_observables: The target observable L, which is an operator induced by randomized multipartite Pauli schemes, and the spectral gap of the observable.
important_parameters: Sample complexity N scaling polynomially with n and epsilon^-1; precision epsilon; and the spectral gap delta which scales as Omega(n^-2).
main_assumptions: The protocol assumes device-independent (DI) settings where devices are black boxes, requires trusted classical inputs, and is specifically focused on n-qubit states (excluding highly structured states like GHZ).
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates the device-independent lifting of a randomized Pauli measurement protocol; Figure 2 shows the physical architecture of the network, the CHSH test implementation, and the transpose-braiding setup.
paper_structure: The paper identifies the scalability barrier and the local-transpose obstacle, introduces a new transpose-braiding method and a teleportation-based measurement scheme, proves the efficiency and robustness of the protocol for n-qubit states, and discusses cryptographic applications and future extensions.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant for quantum networks and distributed quantum computing, as it provides a scalable way to certify large-scale quantum resources without trusting the underlying hardware.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Device-independent lifting of a randomized multipartite Pauli measurement protocol, illustrated for three qubits. Left: In each round, the parties measure ρA in a chosen Pauli basis P0P1 · · · Pn−1 and obtain outcomes b0b1 · · · bn−1. Right: Our protocol lifts this device-dependent Pauli-measurement protocol into its DI version by introducing n auxiliary parties. In the DI implementation, both the underlying states and the measurement devices are treated as uncharacterized black boxes with only classical inputs and outputs.

FIG. 2: Self-testing protocol for implementing randomized multipartite Pauli measurement schemes. (a) The protocol introduces n auxiliary parties and 2n −1 additional Bell pairs, with n pairs between (Al, Bl) and n −1 pairs between neighboring main parties. Together, these resources enable DI lifting of randomized multipartite Pauli measurements. (b) CHSH tests certify the auxiliary Bell pairs and the relevant Pauli observables up to a local complex-conjugation freedom, which manifests as an undetermined sign flip for Y measurements.
